INTRODUCTION .
Thisbook contains, in Part I, all the statutespassed
priorto1893,with an index. These older laws have been
superseded,buthistoricallythey are of importance,besides
theyareusefulfor comparison and there are decisions
construingsome of them which aid in the interpretation
of similarstatutesnow in force ; and old policies written
priorto1893aregovernedby them .
PartII comprisesall the insurance statutes now in
force,withamendments, the originalor repealed section
alsobeinggiven. These statutesare annotated by refer-
encestodecisionsand followed by a complete index.
Chronologicalarrangement of the statutes has been
adoptedinboth Part I and Part II, and adhered to except
whenitwas necessaryto place an amendment near the
originallaw or forsome other apparent specialreason.
PartIII containsdigestsof all the decisionsof the
AppellateCourtsof Kentucky divided into life, fireand
otherkindsof insurance so that the cases relating to each
subjectareplacedtogetherand arranged chronologically
intheseseveraldivisions. It is followed by an index
coveringcompletelyallthecases.
The indexes to the statutes and the decisions have re-
ceivedparticularattentionand much efforthas been ex-
pendedupon them to make them full, plain, compre-
hensiveandpracticallyusefulto lawyers, insurancecom-
panies,agentsand otherswho may be interestedin the
subjectof insurance.
We expressour thanks to Mr. George A. Lewis , of
Frankfort, Ky ., for according us the privilegeof freely
usingwhat we chosein the Kentucky Law Reporter, and
alsotoMr.E. C. Underwood ,of the LouisvilleBar ,for as-
sistanceupon the cases, and also to the West Publishing
xxxiii

INSURANCE LAW OF KENTUCKY .
Company , of St.Paul , Minn . The publicationsof thelat-
ter company were of great service to us, and we have
used with littlechange some of their head notes in the
lateryears, but particularlyhave we derived advantage
from the thoroughly thought-out and so far unequaled
arrangement , analysisand divisionof topics adopted by
that company . Our indexes, however , are alphabetical,
and many catch words as well as general and particular
heads , references and cross-references occur in the en-
deavor to make it easy to find what is sought by a busy
lawyer or by a layman .
We trust that actual experience will realize what has
been our purpose and hope, that this book may be of
practicaluse and permanent value.
March ,1905.
W. W. THUM ,
FREDERICK P. CALDWELL .

COMMENT - STATUTE LAW .
In Kentucky, under the statutesin force, set out in
PartII of thisbook, individuals, partnerships and cor-
porationsengaged in the business of writing insurance
areincludedunder the general words "insurance com-
pany" and aregovernedby the law relatingto insurance
companies. As every individual or partnership may
enterthebusiness, and as the law confersno privileges
otherthan thoseenjoyed by individuals, a franchise tax
isnotimposedupon insurance corporations doing busi-
nessinthisState,exceptupon guarantee and surety com-
panies. The manner in which insurance companies are
formedand the laws and regulations under which they
existare contained in Part II. Although slight conflicts
betweensome sectionsapparently occur, while others are
atfirstnot absolutelyplain, they, in the main , compose
an intelligible, clearand consistentbody of laws, and
usageunderthem, and the decisionsof the courts have
markedout a practicableand serviceableconstruction.
No doubta more comprehensivestatutemight, and prob-
ablywill,beforemany years,be enacted, but at present
thereseems to be no urgent demand or need therefor.
TheReportof Commissioner of Insurance , Henry R. Prew-
itt,covering1904,issued1905 (Geo.G. FetterCo.) makes
norecommendationof a revisionof our laws,but his prac-
ticalsuggestionsfor certainamendments and additions
meritimmediate attention. It might also be to the
interestof domestic companies, as well as of policy-
holders, to enact a law requiring the
thesecuritiesrepresentingit to be depositedwith the
StateTreasurer, he to certifythe fact of depositupon
eachpolicy.

In this State alldescriptionsand statements are held
representationsand not warranties. Policiesare valued
according to the "Combined Experience" or "Actuaries"
table rate of mortality, with interestat 4 per cent per
annum . Foreign companies must consent that serviceof
process upon any agent of such company in thisStateor
upon the Commissioner of Insurance in this State shall
be valid . Transfer to the Federal Court is forbidden on
penalty of revocationof license, and the constitutionality
of the law has been upheld by the Court of Appeals of
Kentucky , though an appeal to the Supreme Court of the
United Statesis pending. A "valued policylaw" isin
force. The limitationof actions upon policiesis fifteen
years, by a recentdecision, notwithstandinga provision
for a shorterlimitationin the policy. For detailsregard-
ing organizationof domestic companies, regulationof
both foreign and domestic companies, publicationof
assetsand liabilities, deposits, annual statements, cer-
tificates, licenses, examinations, fees, agents, taxesand
penalties, rebates, discriminations, etc., referenceshould
be had to the Acts contained in Part II and to the Index
to Part II . An effort has been made to make this Index
full and thorough .

Fire Insurance Notes . It was held in the case of Phoenix Insur-
ance Company v . Carnahan , 63 O. St. 258, that a denial of liability
was not a waiver of an appraisement unless there existed such circum-

appraisement would be fruitless.
In many of the States the requirements as to furnishing a state-
ment or proof of loss under a fire policy are enforced with more strict-
ness than in Kentucky . See Clement on "Fire Insurance as a Valid
Contract ," Chapter 4. Slight circumstances are often held sufficient
to constitute a waiver of statement or proofs of loss. Ib., Rule 62
and authorities cited, pages 234 , 235, 236 and 237 .
So the iron safe clause is held reasonable and enforcible in many
States, though practicallyinoperativein Kentucky . Ib., page 264.
Fraud or false swearing is held to avoid a policy whether before
or after a loss. A fraudulent intent must exist. Ib., pages 274,
275, 276 .
Fraud vitiatesthe entirecontract, not merely the particularitem
to which it referred . Ib ., page 285 and authorities cited .
When there is no statuteprescribinga standard form of policythe
option to rebuild in case of total loss is inoperative . Ib., page 302
and authorities cited .
Where the loss is as great or greater than the amount of insur-
ance , the question of contribution or apportionment does not arise.
Ib., page 308 and authorities cited.
If the company does everything required by the policy and isguilty of no fault whatever and the apprasement does not take place
for some otherreason than the faultof the company , then itistheduty of the insured to take new steps to have an appraisement under
the policy before bringing suit. Rule 35 and authorities cited. Ib.,
pages 159-160. But the company may waive appraisement by not ask-
ing for itor otherwise. Ib., Rule 39, and authoritiescited, page 161.

part of the company it has been held to relieve the insured of com

Waiver- accidentinsurance- agent. It has been held

woman againstlossof time and so agreed with her when

insuredfemalesagainstdeath only.84
In general, the solicitingagent of a firecompany has
much largerpowers than those of a lifecompany , and
thisshouldbe so on the principlethat unless limitations
on agents' powers are known to those dealing with him
hemay be supposed to possess the powers incidentalto
andnecessaryinrespecttomatterscommitted tohiscare.
Thesolicitingagent of a firecompany may in general ac-
ceptorcanceltherisk. The solicitingagent of a lifecom-
panypossessesno such power but must send his applica-
tionto the company . Therefore, the insured has the
righttodealwith agentsaccordinglyand if unadvised to

The thrivingrivertrafficof the State of Kentucky in
early times provided a number of marine decisions.

loss and abandonment . A cargo was sunk and an
effort to recover it would have been made but for

any pro rata of the net proceeds of that portion of the
property that was not insured.33 On accepting abandon-
ment , the insurer becomes liableas for a totalloss. In
one case the insuredabandoned the propertyto the in-
surers, which the latterrefused. They finallytook pos-
sessionof it and theirpossessionwas held to be that of
insurersand not salvors, they to account for a totalloss.33

X. Presumption . It is not a presumption that the officersof a
benefit society have acted with entire regularity and legality in mak-
ing an assessment so as to make it valid. (Case 195.) It will be pre-
sumed in an action on a certificate that the society had collectedthe
full amount assessed unless it be averred to the contrary or unless
it be alleged that it made all reasonable efforts so to do and failed.
(Case 248.)
Y. Materiality of misrepresentations is generally for the court
where the facts are ascertained and undisputed . (Case 207.)
Z. It is for the jury to decide whether the death of the insured
occurred before the expiration of the period covered by insurance
and for which insurance premiums had been paid, the insuredhav-
ing disappeared more than seven years previously. (Kendrick v.A.
O. U. W. , 8 R. 149. )
A. Also ,the question of the sanity of an insured who killedhim-
self is for the jury . (Case 258.)
B. And the question as to whether an insured was suffering
from delirium from illness at the time he shot himself was for the
jury. (Case 260.)
C. If it be alleged in the petition and denied in the answer that
the plaintiffsare the beneficiaries named and no proof be introduced
except the policy itselffiledwith the petition, the plaintiff'scase is
made out and the burden of showing the negative is on the defend-
ant . (Case 52.)
D. Parties. In 1854 it was held that an insured may sue either
set of insurers, leaving them to seek contributionamong themselves.
(Case 272.) But policy provisions generally obligatecompanies only
to a pro rata amount .
E. A master having made a contract for the benefit of the
servant may sue without joining the servant as the trustee of an
express trust. (Case 524.)

STATEMENT .
Theselawsembraced in Part I are now supersededby
thelatergeneralenactmentspassed in 1893, the repealing
clausebeingfound in Paragraph 273, Chapter 171 of the Acts
of1891-2-3(Section 167 herein), as follows: " All general
lawsrelatingto the matters treated of in this chapter shall
standrepealedwhen this Act takes effect it is
enactedthat this Act shall take effect from and after its
passage." (April5, 1893.)
* * * *
Among the matters treatedof in said Chapter 171 are the
InsuranceLaws of 1893 comprisedin Part IIherein.
But specialActs, such as charters, are not deemed
repealedand in another case where doubt exists a note has
been made . See Index No. I under head of
“
Repeal ."
Notwithstandingthe factthat the laws in Part I have been
superseded, they are valuablebecausethey govern any still
existingcontractmade under them, and are alsovaluablefor
purposesofcomparisonwith the presentlaws servingto help
interpretthem. Besides some of them have been construed
bytheCourt of Appeals in decisionsupon questionswhich
ariseinsubstanceunderthe presentlaws.

ularActs grouped under them respectively. For aidinminute

ence must be had to Index I and to the text of the Acts them-
selves .

A. A policy payable to insured's wife and children"or their
representatives," afterthe death of the wife and children,one ofwhom
left a child, inures to the benefit of such surviving child, to the ex-
clusion of a niece to whom the insured attempted to assign the policy.

heirs, can not defeat theirright by devising the fund to his wife,but
she, having paid the premiums , is entitledto be repaid the amount
thereof . Weisert v . Muehl , 81 Ky . 336 .
C. Right to assign certificate in benevolent societies. Basye v .

Bibber v.Van Bibber, 82 Ky . 347, 6 R. 393; Duvall v. Goodson ,79 Ky .
224 , 1 R. 319 .
D. Rights of beneficiaries of insolvent debtor as against creditors.

v . Hartford Life Ins .Co. , 90 Ky . 101 , 11 R. 924 ; Goodpaster v . Conner ,

(Act Dec.3,1892.)
§ 166. Liabilityon fireand storm risksforfullvalueof
propertyas fixedinpolicy.
Acts 1891-93,ch.105, §1,p.368. ApprovedDe-
cember 3,1892.
That insurance companies that take fireor storm risks
on realpropertyin thisCommonwealth shall,on allpoli-
ciesissuedafterthisact takes effect(in case of totalloss
thereofby fireor storm ), be liablefor the fullestimated
value of the property insured, as the value thereofisfixed
in the faceof the policy; and in casesof partiallossofthe
propertyinsured, the liabilityof the company shallnot
exceed the actual loss of the party insured. Provided,
That the estimatedvalueof thepropertyinsuredmay be
diminished to the extent of any depreciationin thevalue
of the property occurring between the dates of thepolicy
and the loss,and provided further, that the insuredshall
be liableforany fraud he may practicein fixingthevalue
of the property,if the company be misled thereby.

respect, the officers and directors causing such report or statement
to be publishedor given out, or assenting thereto, shallbe jointlyand
severally individually liable for any loss or damage resulting there-
from. Acts 1891-93, ch. 171, §12, p. 617, became a law April 5,1893.

§620, Note 4. § 551, Ky . Stats. Directors- number of manner
ofelecting- stock to be owned by .
The affairsof each corporation shall be managed by a board of
not less than three directors, each of whom shall own in his own
rightnot less than three shares of capital stock ; they shall hold
officeuntil their successors are respectively elected and qualified,
and a majority of them shall constitute a quorum for the transaction
ofbusiness. All elections for directors shall be by ballot, and shall be
held in this State; and , in the first instance , the directors shall be
electedat a meeting held before the corporation is authorized to
commence business, and thereafter at an annual meeting of the
stockholders to be held on the day named in the by -laws , and which
shallnot be changed within sixty days next before the day on
which the election is to be held ; and notice of any change shall be
given to each stockholder twenty days before the election is held ;
and if, for any cause, an election is not held on the day named in the
by-laws, a special meeting for that purpose shall be called within
thirtydays thereafter, of which due notice shall be given to each
stockholderin person or by letter mailed to his last known address .
A stockholdermay vote at any meeting by proxy , in writing , signed
by him , and attested in such manner as the by -laws may prescribe ;
and a vacancy in the board of directors shall be filledby the board ,
and the directorsso appointed shall hold officeuntil the next annual
election. The directors of any corporation may , by a vote of the
stockholders, be divided into one , two or three classes, the term of
officeof those of the first class to expire at the annual election next
ensuing,of the second class one year thereafter, and of the third class
two years thereafter; and at each annual election held after such
classification, directors shall be chosen for two or three years , as
the case may be , to succeed those whose terms expire . But each
directorof a banking , trust or insurance company , or building and
loan association, must own in his own right five shares of capital
stock, and a majority of them must be residents of Kentucky during
theirterms of office: Provided , however , That the above -mentioned
clause, declaring that all elections for directors shall be by ballot,
and shallbe held in this State , shall not apply to corporations created
and organized for educational purposes only , and having no capital
stock, and in which tuition to students is free . Acts 1891-93 , ch . 243 ,
§1, p.1257. Approved July 1, 1893. (Being amendment of Act April
5,1893.)

ofvotesthat may be cast.

to vote the shares so held , and persons whose stock is pledged shall
be entitledto vote, unless the right to vote be expressly given in
writingto the pledgee; and in all elections for directors or managers
of any corporation each stockholder shall have the right to cast
as many votes in the aggregate as he shall be entitled to vote , mul-
tipliedby the number of directors or managers to be elected at such

§ 623, Note 2. § 564, Ky . Stats. Shares may be dividedinto
classes- rights of each class.
Acts 1904 , ch. 105 , p . 257 , approved March 25 , 1904 .
Any corporation organized under this law may divide its shares
into classes, such as preferred , common and deferred shares, or as
may be otherwise designated , and it may give to each of the several
classes such priority of right in the payment of the dividends and
in the redemption of the shares , as may be prescribed in the rules
and regulations adopted by the shareholders . But no preferred stock
shall be issued except for cash or its equivalent , nor for less than the
par value of the shares , which shall be stated in the certificatesrepre-
senting the preferred and common stock respectively. Any such cor-
poration , all of whose outstanding stock is common stock, may , by a
resolution adopted by the vote of the holders of not less than two-
thirds in amount of its outstanding capital stock , cast in person or
by proxy , at a special meeting of stockholders called for the purpose
and of which notice shall have been given as provided in the by -laws
of the company , at least twenty days before the date of the meeting,
or at the annual meeting of the stockholders of the company , or by
the written consent of the holders of not less than two- thirds in
amount of its capital stock , distribute or convert its outstanding capi-
tal stock into preferred and common stock in such proportion as shall
be fixed by such resolutionor written consent; Provided, that all
holders of stock of the company at the time of such distributionshall
be entitled to the same pro rata proportions of such preferred and
common stock. And by a resolutionadopted by the likevote or by
such written consent , the capital stock of any corporation may be
increasedand the increased stock may be common or preferredstock,
or partly one and partly the other , as may be fixed by such resolution
or written consent . Any such preferred stock hereinbefore referred
to shall be entitled to receive quarterly , semi -annual or annual divi-
dends thereon at such rate as may be prescribed by the resolutionor
written consent under which the same was issued, and such dividends
shall be payable , as provided in the said resolution or written con-
sent, before any dividends shall be declared on the common stock;
and on the dissolution of the company , voluntarily or otherwise, the
holders of preferred stock shall be entitled to have their shares
redeemed at par before any distribution of any part of the assets
of the company shall be made to the holders of the common stock.

stock .

senting two-thirds of its capital stock , and after notice of the pro-
posed increase or decrease has been mailed to the address of each
stockholder at least twenty days before the meeting is held for that
purpose ; and a statement of the increase or reduction shall be signed
and acknowledged by the president and a majority of the directors,
and filed and recorded in the same manner as articles of incorpora-
tion; but no increase of the capital stock of a banking or trust com-
pany shall be valid until the amount thereof has been bona fidesub-
scribed, and one -half thereof actually paid in, and the remainder shall
be paid in within one year. Acts 1891-93, ch.171, §16.

corporationinto the new , with such other detailsand provisionsas

§ 642, Note 3. § 556, Ky . Stats. Rights and liabilitiesofconsoli-
dated corporation .
When the agreement is signed , acknowledged and recorded in
the same manner as articles of incorporation are required to be,
the separate existence of the constituent corporations shail cease
and the consolidated corporations shall become a single corporation
in accordance with the said agreement , and subject to all the pro-
visions of this chapter and other laws relating to it, and shallbe
vested with all the rights, privileges, franchises, exemptions,
property , business , credits, assets and effects of the constituent
corporations without deed or transfer, and shall be bound for
all their contracts and liabilities: Provided , That no con-
solidated company formed under this chapter , or the laws of this
State , shall be required to pay any organization tax, except that,ifin
the consolidation the capital stock of the consolidated company be
increased , or be thereafter increased to an amount exceeding the
aggregate capital stock of the constituent companies at the time of the
consolidation , then in that event the consolidated company shallnot
have or exercise any corporate powers until it shall have paid intothe
State treasury one-tenth of one per centum upon the amount of said
increase, and upon such payment shall have filed a statement thereof
with the Secretary of State; if, however , any corporationhereafter
formed in another State where no organization tax was requiredto
be paid by it shall be consolidated with one formed in this State,
then the organization tax required by the laws of this State shallbe
paid upon so much of the capital stock of the consolidated corpora-
tion as shall be equal to the capital stock of the foreign constituent
corporation ; or if such foreign corporation hereafter formed may
have been required by the laws of its State to pay an organization
tax less than that then required to be paid in this State, then upon
such consolidation with a corporation of this State an organization
tax shallbe paid equal to the differencebetween that requiredofsuch
foreign corporation in the State of its creation and that which would
have been required had it been formed in this State .

of, or the rights or remedies of the creditors thereof , or of persons

§642,Note 7. § 561, Ky . Stats. Termination of existence of cor-
poration- how effected .
Any corporation organized under this chapter may , by the con-
sentin writing of the owners of the majority of its shares of stock ,
unlessotherwise provided in the articles of incorporation or amend-
ments thereto, close its business and wind up its affairs; and when
any corporationexpires by the terms of the articles of incorporation ,
or by the voluntary act of its stockholders , it may thereafter con-
tinueto act for the purpose of closing up its business , but for no
other purpose; and it shall be the duty of the officers to settle up

weeks, in some newspaper printed and published in the county , if
any,of the fact that it is closing up its business ; and all debts and
demands against the corporation shall be paid in full before the offi-
cersreceiveanything . Acts 1891-93, ch. 171, §24 , p . 621 , became a law
April 5, 1893.

§ 642, Note 9. § 563, Ky . Stats. Sale of under decree- requi-
sites of .
Sales of the property and franchises of corporations that may
be sold under decree of court shall be made after such notice of
the time and place as the court may deem proper ; and if such sales
are made in the foreclosure of one or more mortgages or deeds of
trust, the court may order such sale to be made for the whole amount
of the outstanding bonds and interest secured by such deeds of trust
or mortgage ; or if the property and franchises will produce so much ,
then for the amount of interest due under said deed or deeds of
trust or mortgage , or any of them , subject to the payment by the
purchaser of the outstanding bonds and interest secured thereby as
they become due ; and in the latter event may , by proper orders,
secure the assumption thereof by the purchaser ; but where a sale
shall be ordered to be made , subject as aforesaid, the court shall
direct the officermaking such sale, in the event that the property and
franchises offered do not sell for enough to pay the amount aforesaid,
to sell same free from incumbrances . Sales under this section shall
be made on such credits as the court may deem proper . Acts 1891-
93 , ch. 171 , §26 , pp . 622 , 623 , became a law April 5, 1893 .
§ 642,Note 10. § 566, Ky . Stats. Organization- want of legalnot
a defense .
No corporation organized under this chapter shall be permitted
to set up or rely upon the want of legal organization as a defense to
any action against it; nor shall any person transacting business with
such corporation , or sued for injury done to its property , be permitted
to rely upon such want of legalorganizationas a defense. Acts1891-
93 , ch. 171 , §29 , pp . 623, 624 , became a law April 5, 1893 .
§ 642, Note 11. § 568, Ky . Stats. Stock or bonds may not be
issued except for value .
No corporation shall issue stock or bonds except for an equivalent
in money paid or labor done , or property actuallyreceivedand ap-
plied to the purposes for which such corporation was created, and
neither labor nor property shall be received in payment of stock
or bonds at a greater value than the market price at the time said
labor was done or property delivered ; and all fictitiousincrease of
stockshallbe void. Acts 1891-93, ch. 171, §31, p. 624, became a law
April 5, 1893. (Identicalwith §193 present (1891) Constitutionof
Kentucky , p. 142 , Ky . Stats . (Ed . 1903 ).)

come detrimental to the interest and welfare of the Commonwealth or

to institutesuch proceedings as may be proper and necessary to have

of such corporation. Acts 1891-93, ch. 171 , §32 , p . 624 , became a law
April 5, 1893.
This is in accordance with $ 205 of the present (1891 ) Constitu-

§ 642,Note 13. § 570,Ky . Stats. Constitution- provisions of to be
accepted.
No law shall be passed for the benefit of, or in the interest of,
any corporationheretofore created or organized by or under the laws
of thisState or any other State ; nor shall any corporation avail itself
of the provisions of this chapter , unless such corporation shall have
previously, by a resolution adopted by its board of directors and filed
in the officeof the Secretary of this State , accepted the provisions of
the Constitutionof this State; and such resolution, or a certifiedcopy
thereof, shall be evidence for and against such corporation . Acts
1891-93,ch.171, §33, pp. 624, 625, became a law April 5, 1893.
§190 of the present (1891 ) Constitution reads as follows : No
corporation in existence at the time of the adoption of this Constitu-
tion shall have the benefit of future legislation without first filing in
theofficeof the Secretary of State an acceptance of the provisions of
thisConstitution. Ky . Stats., p . 142 .
§ 642, Note 14. § 573, Ky . Stats . Charters in conflict with this
chapter- provisions relating to- penalty .
The provisions of all charters and articles of incorporation ,
whether granted by special act of the General Assembly or obtained
under any general incorporation law , which are inconsistent with the
provisionsof this chapter concerning similar corporations , to the ex-
tentof such conflict, and allpowers , privilegesor immunities of any
such corporation which could not be obtained under the pro-
visionsof this chapter, shall stand repealed on September 28, 1897 ;
and ifthe officers, managers or agents of such corporation shall, after
saiddate, exercise any powers , privileges or immunities repealed by
thissection or inconsistent with the provisions of this chapter , re-
latingto similar corporations, or which could not be obtained under
thischapter, the officer, manager or agent so offending, and the cor-
porationfor which he acts, shall each be guilty of a misdemeanor , and
fined for each offense not less than one hundred nor more than one
thousand dollars, and upon the conviction of the corporation , the
trialjury may , at their discretion, direct the forfeiture of its charter
orarticlesof incorporation, in which case the court shall so adjudge.
Afterthe twenty-eighth day of September , 1897, the provisions of
thischapter shall apply to all corporations created or organized un-
der the laws of this State, if said provisions would be applicable to
them if organized under this chapter . Acts 1891-93, ch. 171 , §36 , p .

First enacted in 1893 .
1. Rebates .
A company may pay an agent for services by issuing a policy on
hislife,butifitagreesto pay him more for his servicesthan they are
worth,with design to give him a rebate on the premium , it is a viola-
tionof8656,Ky .Stats. EquitableLife Assur . Society v. Com .113 Ку .
126,23 R. 2359, 67 S. W. 388 .
2. Section held constitutional .
This sectionis not unconstitutional as being in restraint of trade
or preventingcompetition. Equitable Life Assur . Society v. Com .,
23R. 2359,67 S. W. 388.
3. Policymust containentirecontract.
The policy must contain the entire contract , and unless the appli-
cationis attached, statements therein can not be relied on to prevent
a recoveryon the policy. Manhattan Life Ins. Co. v. Myers , 109 Ky .
372,22 R. 875,59 S. W. 30; Provident Savings Life Assur . Society v.
Puryear'sAdmr .,109 Ky .381, 22 R. 980,59 S. W. 15; FidelityMut . Life
Ins.Co. v.Price, 25 R. 1148 , 77 S. W. 384. But a note for a premium
providingfor forfeitureof the policy if not paid at maturity , need not
be attachedto the policy. FidelityMut . Life Ins. Co. v . Price, 25 R.
1148,77 S. W. 384.
4. Actions - venue - limitation .
The circuitcourt of the county where the offense is committed
has jurisdictionof an action to recover the penalty denounced in this
section. Com .v.Long ,17 R. 207, 30 S. W. 628. The limitationof an
actionforsuch penalty is one year. Com . v. Equitable Life Assur .
Soc.,100Ky .341,18 R. 778, 38 S. W. 491.

go in fullto person with insurable interest. Beard v . Sharp , 100 Ky .

§712 , KY . STATS. Assessment- actionto recover-

added theretoin addition to lawful interest, as a penalty

quired. Any member who neglectsor refusesto pay his

satisfactoryto the directorsor executive committee, be

mittee,or as the by-laws may prescribe,from thecorpora-
tion; and , when thus excluded, the secretaryshallcancel
or withdraw his policy or policies, and notifyhim of the
fact, which shall prevent him recoveringfor any lossor
damage sustained after such exclusion. Such member
shallremain liablefor the payment of any assessment
made prior to his exclusion, and for penalty above pro-
vided, in case actionhas been or shallbe brought against
him therefor, within twelve months afterthe time itwas
due . The officersof every such corporationmay proceed
to collectany assessment,when due and unpaid forthirty
days ;and refusaland neglecton theirpart to endeavorto
collectsuch assessments,or to perform any of theduties
imposed by thisarticle, shallrender them liable, individ-
ually, for the amount lostby any person who losesby
their neglect or refusal, and an action for same be main-
tainedagainstthem forsuch loss. If any member ofthe
corporation shall be excluded therefrom, as hereinpro-
vided,and the policyissuedto him canceled,the secretary
shallat once enter the same , with date thereof,upon the
records of the association, and eitherinform in personor
by mail notifythe member of such exclusion; and ifby
mail the postageshall be prepaid, and the noticebe
addressedto theofficegiven in the applicationor policy;

PART II. LAWS 1893-1904 , INCLUSIVE .
Municipal Taxation.
The two following sections were repealed by the Act of 1902 :
§ 4231, Ky . Stats. (1899.) Taxation of foreign insurance com-
paniesother than lifeand assessmant .
Every insurance company , other than lifeinsurance companies and
assessment casualty companies , not organized under the laws of this
State,but doing business therein, shall, on the firstday of July in each
year, or within thirty days thereafter, return to the Auditor of Public
Accounts, for deposit in the Insurance Department , a statement under
oath, of allpremiums received in this State , or out of this State , on
business done in this State during the year ending on the thirtieth
day of June last preceding or since the last returns were made , and
shallgive the name and location of, and amount of premiums received
by,each agent, and losses paid at each agency , and shall, at the same
time, pay into the State Treasury a tax of two dollars upon each one
hundred dollars of said premiums so ascertained ; less returned pre-
miums on canceled policies and re-insurance in companies having au-
thorityto transact business in this State , and upon payment , file a
statement thereof with the Secretary of State . Acts 1891-2-3, Art . XI ,
§ 5, p. 349. (See § 4234 now in force ).
Aetna Life v. Coulter, 25 R. 193 .
§4233, Ky . Stats. (1899.) Penalty for failingto report and pay
taxes- duty of Commissioner .
Any insurance company failing or refusing for thirty days to
return the statements required , under the oath of some principal
officeror general agent or manager of the State , and to pay the tax
hereinrequired,shallforfeitone hundred dollarsfor such offense, and
it shall be the duty of the Insurance Commissioner to revoke the
authorityto such company or its agents , and to publish such revocation
in some newspaper of this Commonwealth . Acts 1891-2-3, Art. XI ,
§7,p. 350. (Same as § 4230 now in force).

An actwas passed in March ,1902,authorizingthe sub-
missionto the voters of the State of Kentucky of an
amendment to Section 181 of the present (1891) Consti-
tution. The amendment was submitted and carried. It
read as follows :
"Constitution. §181. The General Assembly shall
notimposetaxesforthe purposesof any county,city,
town or other municipal corporation, but may , by
generallaws,conferon theproperauthoritiesthereof,
respectively, the power to assess and collecttaxes.
The General Assembly may , by general laws only,
provideforthepaymentoflicensefeeson franchises,

stock used for breeding purposes, the varioustrades,
occupations and professions, or a specialor excise
tax; and may , by general laws, delegatethe power to
counties, towns , citiesand other municipal corpora-
tions, to impose and collectlicensefeeson stockused
for breeding purposes, on franchises, trades, occupa-
tions and professions. And the General Assembly
may , by general laws only, authorizecitiesor towns
of any classto providefor taxationfor municipal
purposes on personal property, tangibleand intangi
ble, based on income, licensesor franchises,in lieu
ofan ad valoremtax thereon. Provided,Citiesofthe
firstclassshallnot be authorized to omit the imposi
tion of an ad valorem tax on such property of any
steam railroad, streetrailway, ferry, bridge, gas,
water,heating, telephone,telegraph,electriclightor
electricpower company .” Acts 1902, ch. 50, pages
100, 101. (The italicizedportion was added to
original$181 by the amendment and the word "such"
omitted from the fifth line above before the word
" taxes." )
By saidamendment theGeneralAssembly was author-
izedby general laws to authorize" citiesor towns of any
classto providefor taxationfor municipalpurposeson
personalproperty, tangibleand intangible, basedon in
come , licensesor franchisesin lieu of an ad valoremtax
thereon," etc. In pursuance of the amendment,an act
was passedby theGeneralAssembly,approvedMarch 18,
1904 (seeActs of1904,Chap .33,pages93 to99),bywhich
citiesof the firstclass were authorized to avail themselves
of saidconstitutionalamendment and to passtheneces
sary ordinancesto thatend. Said act alsoprovidesthat
an insuranceadjusterin saidcityshouldbe requiredto
pay a licenseof not lessthan $100 nor more than$500
per annum ,and thata solicitingagentshouldbe required
to pay a licenseof not lessthan $10 nor more than$500
per annum . That act, in pursuance of the saidamend-

ment to Section 181, repealed and substituted Sections

under the new Section 181 of the Constitution . And

1905 .
It is to be noted that from the old Section 3011 of the
KentuckyStatutes, the followingwords and sentences
were dropped :
"Every life,fireor accident, casualty and indem-
nityinsurance company , titlecompany , titleinsur-
ancecompany or abstractcompany ,doing business in
saidcityshall,on or before the firstday of February
of each year, pay to the sinking fund not less than
two nor more than threedollarson every hundred dol-
larsofpremiums receivedon businessdone in the city
duringthe previousyear."
and are not to be found in the substituted Section 3011 .
Thus,itisno longerobligatoryon the cityto levy that
amount of tax on premiums . It is further to be noted
that this section was held constitutional in the case of
Fidelityand CasualtyCo. v. City of Louisville, 20 R.
1785,50 S.W. 35,and held to be lawfulas a licensetax
inadditionto an ad valorem tax . See-
German Washington Mutual Fire Ins. Co. v.
CityofLouisville,25 R. 1697,78 S.W. 472.
German NationalIns.Co.v.Cityof Louisville,
21 R. 1179,54 S. W. 732 .

able licensetax in lieu of allad valorem taxes on person-
altyfor citiesof the firstclass.

PART III.
DECISIONS .
I.
LIFE INSURANCE DECISIONS .
Case1. New York LifeIns.Co.v.Graham ,2 Duvall,506
(1866).
Robertson, J., affirming.
Actionon LifePolicy- BurdenofProof. In an action on a life
insurancepolicy,where the defensereliedon isa falserep-
resentation as to the habits of the insured in the use of
intoxicatingliquors, and that his death was caused by in-
temperance,theburden of proof ison the defendant.
Case2. St.LouisMutualLifeIns.Co.v.Graves,6 Bush,
268 (1869 ).
Williams, C. J., and Robertson, J., reversing.
Insanity- Suicide. A condition in a policy, that if the
partyshall"dieby his own hand " the policyshallbe void,
refersto a voluntary self-destruction, and not to suicide
while insane .
Insanity- kindsof. There are two kinds of insanity- intel-
lectualand moral. "When , as oftenhappens from con-
genitalmal-organizationor supervenientdisturbanceof
thenormal conditionof a sound mind in a sound body ,the
sensespresentfalseimages which , accredited necessarily
by thedeludedvictim as intuitivecertainties,no reasoning
orproofcan rectifythe illusionof a mind in such abnor-
malcondition,and consequently,as no punitarysanction
canpreventthe effectsof such insane delusion,there isno
325

INSURANCE LAW OF KENTUCKY .
Life Insurance Decisions.
legalresponsibility. This is calledintellectualinsanity."
"But , while the senses are apparently sound and true,
the affectionsmay be perverted, or the moral sentiments
unhinged in such a degree as to subjugate the willtosome
morbid appetiteor ungovernable passion,and thus precipi-
tatesagainstthe willinsane but consciouswrong . Thisis
contradistinctivelycalledmoral insanity. Such are the
forms of monomania entitledkleptomania, pyromania,
nymphomania ,homicidalmania,etc.,now welldefinedand
recognizedas irresponsibleinsanity." These definitions
have not been judiciallyapplied.
Insanity- Evidence. The opinion of unprofessionalwit-
nessesas to whether insured, under allthe circumstances,
ifsane,would have shot himself,are not competenton the
question of his insanity.
Evidence that a recent rumor was in circulation ac-
cusing a man of crime should not be submitted to prove
thatbecauseof such rumor he took hislife,unlessitbe
also proved that he heard the rumor .
Policy- Construction. The condition as to death "by his
own hand" reasonablyimports, therefore, thatifthein-
suredshouldcommit suicidevoluntarilywhen he hadthe
moral power to forbear,justas he might commit itby the
habitualuse of opium or intoxicatingliquors, thepolicy
should be thereby avoided. The death in such casesalike
must be voluntaryact of a sane mind and a responsible
will. As policiesarepeculiarintheirstyleand noteasily
intelligibleto the common mind , thislanguageshouldin
cases of doubt be favorably construed for the benefitof
the assured .
Insanity. A conditionin a policy that ifthe partyshall
" die by his own hand " the policy shallbe void,refersto
a voluntary self-destructionand not to a self-killingwhile
insane .
Cited : 112 Ky . 460 .
Case 3. St. LouisMutualLifeIns.Co.v.Kennedy,6
Bush,450 (1869).
Williams , C. J., reversing.
Premium- PaymentofbyNote.Where a policystipulates
thatitshallnot takeeffectuntilthe premium ispaidin

PART III - DECISIONS .
Life Insurance Decisions.
thelife-timeand good healthof the applicant,the giving
of a promissory note "payable on the delivery of the
policy" isnot a compliance with the stipulation, itbeing
shown thatthe policy was not deliveredowing to illness
of insured .
Case4. New York LifeIns.Co.v.Clopton,7 Bush,179
(1870).
Robertson, J., affirming.
EffectofWar on LifeInsuranceContract. The existence of a
stateof war did not dissolve or render void a life insur-
ance contract issued from New York on the life of a non-
combatantresidingin Virginia.
Non-PaymentofPremium on AccountofWar . The assured paid
regularlyfor four years, but failedto pay for the years
1862,1863 and 1864. During theseyears the law deprived
thecompany of the right to receivepremiums from the
assured,who livedin Virginia, the home of the company
beingin New York . The failurethus to pay these pre-
miums should not avoid or forfeitthe policy.
War SuspendsPerformanceandRemedy. The parties being
technicallyenemies, businessand commercial dealings
were prohibited, but the contract in question, namely , a
policyof insurance,being made beforethe war began and
beingof a nature which could be performed by a single
payment,the effectof war on such a contractwas to sus-
pend itsperformance and the remedy thereon during the
war .
PolicynotAvoidedby War because not one of ContinuousPerformance.
Intheparticularcase,the policywas forthe entireterm
and thecontractcould be performed by payment of the
stipulatedamount of $5,000 in the event of the loss in-
suredagainst,and the policywas thus not a contractof
continuousperformance, but one which might be per-
formedby a singleact,or periodicalactsbetween which
therewas nothing to perform .
PremiumanEntirety. The premium payable annually was
nevertheless,as a consideration, an entiretyand not in a
legalsensea continuing consideration.
327

INSURANCE LAW OF KENTUCKY ,
Life InsuranceDecisions.
EffectofWar on Agent'sPowers. The war deprivedthelocal
agent of the company from solicitingpolicies. He ,never-
theless,had the power to receivein Virginiapremiumson
such contractsmade theretofore. However , he couldnot
legallyhave paid them to the company in New York.
Cited : 9 Bush , 21 ..
Case 5. Stokes& Son v.Coffey,8 Bush,533 (1871).
Lindsay , J., affirming.
Creditors- Insolvency.Where a person being insolvent
causes a policy of lifeinsurance payable to hisrepre-
sentatives to be canceled and another issued to his wife
instead, such transaction is to be treatedas a voluntary
giftor assignmentof a portionof hisestate,and isvoid
as to antecedent creditors .
RightofInsolventtoInsureLifeforCreditor'sBenefit. A debtor
may prefer one of his creditorsby insuringhislifeinhis
favor ; but such preference can not be allowed to exceed
the amount of the debts due such creditor,and thesurplus
afterpaying such creditorbecomes assetsof thedeceased's
estate .
Right of Insolventto InsureLifefor hisWife'sBenefit. While a
voluntary provisionby an insolventfor hiswifebymeans
of lifeinsurancemay be sustained,yetsuchprovisionwill
not be sustained to the prejudiceof antecedentcreditors,
beyond what is necessary to provide reasonablesupport
forher,and support and education forher family.
Cited : 11 Bush , 570 ; 3 R. 134 .
Case 6. ConnecticutMutual LifeIns.Co.v.Siegel,9
Bush,450 (1872.)
Lindsay , J., affirming.
NoticeandProofofDeath.A requirementin a lifepolicyof
noticeand proof of death does not imply thatthecir
cumstances shallbe setforth;and a noticeand proofisnot
invalidatedby a statement that the assuredsufferedwith
delirium tremens from drink, without any furtherspecifi-
cation to show whether a stipulationavoidingthepolicy
for intemperance had been violated. When an apparent
ground of defense is declared by a separateand unneces-
sary narrationof circumstances,and theproofsrequired
by the policy are complete without that narrationand
328

PART III - DECISIONS .
Life Insurance Decisions.
disclosure, itcan not be said that the party has failedto
comply with the conditionimposed upon his right to liti-
gate his claim .
Case 7. MississippiValleyLifeIns. Co. v. Neyland, 9
Bush,430 (1872).
Lindsay, J., affirming.
Premium- Paymentby Note. Although the policies of an
insurancecompany stipulatethat they shallnot be bind-
ingupon thecompany untilthe premium ispaid in money ,
where the agent issuesa receiptand takes a promissory
note, the company will be bound , it appearing that the
applicantdid not know of any limitationupon the agent's
authorityin that respect.
Evidence- PrivateInstructions.The company has no right to
introduceas evidence to prove the extent of an agent's
authoritya pamphlet containing privateinstructions; nor
isa letterfrom a generalagent of the company to a special
agentadmissiblefor that purpose.
Agents- Power to Waive Conditionsas to Payment of Premium .The
weightofmodern authorityis that a general agent of an
insurancecompany whose businessit is to solicitapplica-
tionsfor insurance and receive the firstpremiums has the
righttowaive the conditionrequiringpayment in money ,
and to acceptthe promissory note of the applicant, or of
a thirdparty in lieuthereof,or to undertake to make the
payment to the company himself , and that when the cash
payment is actuallywaived in eitherof these modes , the
contractbinds the company , notwithstanding the recital
in thepolicythat it is not to be binding until the cash
portionof the firstpremium is actuallypaid in money .
Cited : 107 Ky . 249 .
Case8. St.LouisMutual LifeIns. Co. v. Grigsby, 10
Bush,310 (1874).
Lindsay, J., affirming.
ForfeitureforNon-Payment of Premium- Time oftheEssence. The
conditionsin policiesof lifeinsuranceprovidingforfor-
feituresfornon -payment of premiums in exact accordance
with the terms of the agreement have been upheld and
enforcedby the courts. Such forfeituresare not pen-
329

INSURANCE LAW OF KENTUCKY ..
Life Insurance Decisions.
alties. Time isof the essenceof the contract. They may
be kept in force from year to year at the willof the in-
sured, thisbeing a privilegesecured to the insuredby the
payment of the stipulated premiums according to his
agreement .
Non-PaymentofPremiumNoteForfeitsPolicy. Where ,asa mat-
terof favor to the insured,creditisextended him forsome
portion of a cash payment , the failureto pay the note
representingsuch portion is regarded as a failuretopay
the premium , and the policy will be forfeited. In this
case there had been no such failure .
CommutedPolicy. A forfeitedpolicyof lifeinsurancefor
ten thousand dollars was " renewed and continued in force
for the commuted amount of three thousand dollars until
the 16th day of August ,1871." Held ,that thislimitation
as to time was unavailing,the originalpolicyhaving been
reinstatedto continue in force during the lifeof thein-
sured. Being commuted , it became essentiallya paid-up
policy,except that the company had the right, shouldits
affairsrender it necessary, to demand the payment in
whole, or in part, of the note executedfor the unpaid
portion of the annual premiums .
Cited : 11 Bush , 573 ; 14 Bush 66 ; 2 R. 406 ; 5 R. 278 ; 84 Ky .663;

Forfeiture- Intereston Premium Notes- Dividends- Surplus. A
policy of lifeinsurance contained a provisionthat after

turn to the assured a"due proportionof thesurpluson each
and every year's business, during the continuance of the

of default in payment of any premium , be liableforas
many tenth parts of the originalsum as there had been
complete annual premiums paid at the time of such de-
fault;but itwas furtherstipulatedthat in order tosecure
such proportion of the policy, all premium notes must
be taken up or the interestthereon be paid annuallyin
cash on the date of the annual maturity of the premium
until the notes are canceled by return of the surplusor
the whole policywould be forfeited. It was held thatthe
stipulationfor the cash payment of the annual interest
on the premium notes was not " of the substanceof the
contract." And that although there was a stipulationin
the notes that the "surplus" should be appliedto pay the
notes, where the surplus was not in fact creditedon the
notes or returned to the assured, it would be considered
as applicableto the payment of the interest. And as the
surpluswas sufficientto pay the interest, no forfeiture
occurred .

ing the continuanceof the policy, afterthreefullpremi-

participatingin the dividends; althoughhe was entitled
to have enough of them given to him to pay theinterest
on notesalreadygiven so as to prevent a forfeiture.

Pamphleta PartofContract.When an insurance company ,
throughitschiefofficers,issuesa pamphlet making repre-
sentationsas tothe plans upon which itinsures,and sends
thispamphlet out by itssolicitingagents, it is to be re-
gardedas a part of the contract of insurance entered into
upon the faithof the representationscontained therein,
and isto be considered in connection with the policy in
determining what the contract was .
In thiscase the insured isentitledto a paid-up policy,
althoughhis originalpolicydid not provide therefor,as a
pamphlet issued by the company and exhibited by the
solicitingagent for the purpose of inducing the insured to
takeinsurance,representedthat the company would issue
such a policy.
Paid-Up PolicyafterForfeiture. Where a policy of lifeinsur-
anceprovidesthat afterthe payment of a certainnumber
ofannualpremiums thecompany will, in caseofdefault,
issuea paid-up policy in the event the old policy is sur-
rendered within a certain time after default, the failure
tosurrenderthe old policy within the time provided will
not forfeitthe right to a paid -up policy, and in this re-
spectthereis no distinctionbetween endowment policies
and ordinary lifepolicies.
In this case the time limited for the surrender of the
oldpolicy, which was an endowment policy, was thirty
days. It does not appear that that policy was ever sur-
rendered,or any demand made for a paid-up policy until
about the time this suit was instituted, March 29, 1881,
more than five years after default. Held : That the as-
suredhas not forfeitedhis right to a paid-up policy.

notefor an unpaid premium in considerationof which
therewas a written extension of the policy for twelve
months, with the proviso that if the notes were not paid
atmaturity,the policyshould "at once become void with-
out noticeto the assured", was but the continuation of
theoriginalcontract,and not a new contractwhereby the

inalamount insured, the beneficiarycan eitherreturnthe

on the company afterdefaultis to issuea paid-up policy,

amount . And whether or not,under thatrule,theright
to demand a paid-up policyislostby a failuretoapply
withintheprescribedtime,itmay be barredby thestatute
of limitations .

Case 42. Hise v. HartfordLifeIns.Co.,90 Ky . 101 ; 11
R.924;13 S.W. 367. Hitev.Aetna LifeIns.Co.,Id.(1890).
Bennett, J., affirming.
ProvisionforWifeand Family- EffectofInsolvency. Joseph S.
Hite,now deceased,qualifiedas the guardian,in 1867, of
theappellantsand receivedan estatebelonging to them of
about$13,600, consistingof bonds . Thereafter, in 1868
and 1869, he insured his lifein the two appellatecom-
panies for the benefit of his wife and children in sums
which amounted in the aggregate to about $32,000. He
paidpremiums until1871, inclusive. He then took out a
paid-up policyforthe benefitof his said wife and children.
Two or threeyears thereafterhe became insolvent, and
later, died. He was not insolventwhen he took out the
policies.
Creditorssought to subjectthe insurance on the ground
thattheprovisionfor wife and childrenwas unreasonable
-a gift- and again,that itwas fraudulent. Held : That
itwas a reasonableprovision, free from fraud, and that
theinsuranceshould go to the wife and children. The
Court held that Hite was not insolvent when he took out
thepoliciesand,although indebted,was not so beyond his
abilityto pay, or to the extent of affectinghis credit, or
totheextentof materiallyaffectingthe rightsof creditors
by investingas much as between fiveand six thousand
dollarsin a policyfor wife and children- nine children,
most of whom were infantsand one of whom , an infant,
was a cripple.
The case of Stokes v . Coffee , 8 Bush , 533 (case
5 herein), is approved. That was a case in which
the insurance was also taken prior to the Insur-
anceAct of Kentucky , dated 1870, and in that case
it was held that an insolvent husband might in-
sure his life for the benefit of his wife and children in

a reasonablesum and the same would not be subjecttothe
payment of his debts, provided his wife had not a suffi-
ciency of estateof her own with which to supportherself
and children and to educate the latter .
The caseofThompson v.Cundiff,11 Bush 567 (case10
herein), is also approved , which decided that the Act of
1870,while itdid not controlinsurance theretoforeeffected,
should be receivedas a legislativeinterpretationof such
policiesto the effectthat theywere valid,though made byan
insolventhusband against existingcreditors,to theextent
that the " sum was reasonable " ; that in no case could said
policiesbe successfullyattacked by antecedentcreditors,
except for intentionalfraud, and insurancesby insolvent
husbands for the benefit of their wives and children in un-
reasonable sums would be sufficient evidence of such
fraud, and would subjectthe premiums paidtothedebts
of such creditors .

Forfeiture- Non-PaymentofPremiumNotes. A policy provided
thatif defaultshall be made in the payment of any
premium, the company will pay as many tenth
parts of the original sum assured as there shall
have been complete annual premiums paid at the
time of such default . But in order to secure such
proportionof the policy, all premium notes must be
taken up or the interest thereon paid annually in
cashon the date of the annual maturity of the premium ,
untilthe notes are canceled by returns of surplus , or the
wholepolicywillbe forfeited". The policyholderpaid
thepremiums for two years and the intereston her pre-
mium note for severalyears longer, when she ceased to
make any furtherpayments . It was held that the failure
topay the intereston the premium note did not work a
forfeiture,but thatthe company was liablefortwo-tenths
of the originalsum assured, less the premium note and
unpaid interest thereon .
ConflictofLaws- Limitation. It is provided by chapter 71,
art.4, sec.19, General Statutes (Ky . Stats., sec. 2542 ),
that when a cause of action has arisen in another
Statebetween residentsof such State, or between them
and residents of another State or country , an action
broughtin thisState isgoverned by the statuteof limita-
tionsof the State where the cause of action accrued , and
not by the law of the place where the contract was made .
The statute of limitations of the State where the cause of
actionaccrues governs the time within which suit shall
be brought,although itis provided in the policythat the
demand must be made at the home office of the insurance
company locatedin another State.

no misrepresentationsin an applicationfora policyof

Combinationof insurancecompaniesto keep up rates. Insur-
anceisnot one of the subjectscovered by Sec. 3915 Ky .
Stats., prohibiting combinations for the purpose of con-
trollingor regulatingor fixingthe prices of articlesof
merchandiseor manufacture , or of property, and the in-
dictmentagainst the appellant is not good under the
statute .
Such combination is not indictable under the common law . The
indictment is bad under the common law , as it does not
appearthat in England and prior to the fourth year of
King James IV . there was any statute or law in force
underwhich fixingby agreement or maintaining the rates
of insurance could be deemed to be indictable . Nor has
therebeen any such natural growth or expansion of the
principlesof the common law as would warrant the court
inholdingsuch concerted action or combination indict-
able at common law . It is well settled that such con-
tractsare void and unenforceable. In Kentucky , how-
ever, the law has not gone further than this.

642 ; 20 R. 1442 ; 49 S. W. 425 (1899 ).

Forfeitureof policy- Waiver of- Lapse of policyfornon-paymentof
premium note. Pentecost insured his life in appellant
company for $1,000, and paid the firstyear'spremium.
Wilson , agent of appellant,agreed to take $9.20cashand
three notes for the same amount for the second premium.
When the firstnote felldue , to wit, July 17th,1897,the
insured applied to Wilson , the agent who had been en-
trusted by the company with the collectionof thenote,
for an extensionof time for itspayment . Time was given
until August 1st. The insured failed to pay. Wilson,
agent,returned the note to the company . Afterwards,on
August 30th, a friendof Pentecostasked Wilson, the
agent, ifhe had the note and whether the same couldbe
paid. The agent, Wilson , replied that he had not the
note and that Pentecost could not be reinstated without
a certificateof health. Shortly after,Pentecostdiedand
suitwas brought on the policy and issueswere made as
indicated in the foregoing recitalof facts, and a judg-
ment was rendered for the plaintiff,beneficiary.
Held , that the policy lapsed on August 1st forfailure
to pay the premium note, and that the factthatthecom-
pany did not then notifytheinsuredof thelapsingofhis
policywas to no extenta waiver. The insured,Pente-
cost,was expresslytold in the demand for the paymentof
the note when it approached maturity thatthevalidityof
the insurance depended upon his prompt attentiontothe
payment of the note,and the additionaltime affordedhim
by the agent up to August 1stcan not be reliedupon bythe
beneficiary, and when Pentecostfailedto pay, thenhe
was bound to understand that his policylapsed.

Case97. Schlamp v.Berner'sAdmr .,21 R. 324 ;51 S.
W. 312 (1899).
White, J., affirming.
Insurableinterestnecessaryinanassigneeofpolicy. Mary Ber-
ner took out two policies in the Prudential Life In-
surance Company and assigned them to appellant,
Schlamp, in writing. After the death of the insured, the
heirsof Mary Berner claimed the proceeds, as also did
Schlamp. It is not contended that appellant ever had
any insurable interest in the life of deceased either as
kinswoman or creditor except that appellant was re-
questedto attend to the burial of deceased, and all this
expense has been paid without question . The question
presentedhere is, whether the assignment of these poli-
ciesby the insuredwho paid allthe premiums ,to one who
had no insurableinteresteither as being of kin or cred-
itor,isvalidso as to give the assigneethe proceeds. The
authoritiesthroughout the several States are not uni-
form, but in this State the doctrine is well settledthat
one who had no insurable interest, either as of kin or as
creditor, can derive no benefitfrom lifeinsurance by as-
signmentof the policy,and that as to creditors,his inter-
estcould never exceed his debt. (See also 81 Ky . 368.)

Case100. EquitableLifeAssuranceSocietyv.Spillman,
22 R. 183; 56 S. W. 710 (1900 ).
Paynter,J., reversing.
Tontinepolicy- Paid-up policydenied. Appellee took out a
fifteen-payment policy on the tontine savings plan. The
annualpremium was $197.90. He paid saidamount for
theyears 1881, 1882, 1883, 1884, and 1885, respectively,
but paid nothing for the remaining years. After the ex-
pirationof the fifteenyears, the tontine period, he sued
fora paid-up policy for an amount equal to the sum of
the premiums paid, to-wit, $989.50. It was agreed by
thecontractbetween the partiesthat in considerationof
the issualof the policy upon the tontine savings fund
plan allrights to a paid-up policy under provisions of
Chapter 347, Laws of New York , were waived , also that
"previoustothe completion of itstontinedividend period,
thispolicyhas no surrender value in cash or a paid-up
policy." Held : The language used in the policy ex-
presslyexcludes the idea that a paid-up policy was to
be issuedto the insured. The appellant also executed,
inconnectionwith the deliveryof the policy,a writing as
follows,to wit: "It is hereby expressly agreed that the
amountassured,under policy No. 237,393on the lifeof
R. T. Spillman,may be reducedat any time at thedesire
of saidSpillman, to any less amount , or to an amount
equalto the sum of the premiums paid in on said policy,
the premiums after the reduction being reduced in the
same proportionas the amount assured is reduced, the
fractionalpart of the policy given up being subject to
the same rules of value on surrender as if written on a
separatepolicyof the same kind (tontinesavings fund ).
Further, that number and date of policy shall remain un-
changed." Held , further, That the writing gives the
assuredthe right to have the amount of his policy re-
duced,at any time he might desireit,to any amount less
thanthepolicy, or to an amount equal to the sum of the
premiums paid on it, the premiums , after the reduction
ofthepolicy,being reduced in the same proportion as the
amount of the policy. The language does not seem to
limitthe amount of the reduction to which the appellee

was entitled,except itbe to an amount equal to thesum
of the premiums paid on the policy. When reducedit
was not to be a paid-up policy,for annual premiums were
thereafter to be paid, but in a reduced amount. Re
versed, with directionsto dismiss the petition.

881; 58 S. W. 699 (1900).

Agents- Authoritytowaiveconditionsof policy. On September
4th,1897,one Tweddell applied for a policy of lifeinsur-
ance for $1,000, payable to his wife, and delivered the
applicationto the solicitingagent of appellant. The
amount of the firstpremium was $41.20, of which $22.70

company. Two notes for these respectiveamounts were
drawn up,payable in ninetydays,the one for $22.70to the
solicitingagent and the other, for $18.50, to appellant.
The latterwas signed and delivered. Whether the other
was signedthe evidenceis not clear. It was then agreed
thatthe solicitingagent would take out the $22.70 note
in board at Tweddell'shouse, and the note thereforwas
destroyed, but whether before or after it was signed is
not shown with certainty. The arrangement , however ,
was acceptedby the solicitingagent in payment of the
premium . The application was forwarded to the com-
pany and accepted. The policy issued on September 9th
at the home officeand was forwarded to the State agents
atLouisvillewho then forwarded itto the solicitingagent
fordeliveryand it was by him delivered on September
16th. Tweddell was then dangerously sick of an acute
disorderwhich had come on after the application was
made and of which he died on September 19th. The ap-
plicationcontained the stipulationthat the policy should
nottake effectuntilthe firstpremium was actuallypaid
during the lifetimeand good health of the assured, and
alsocontaininga prohibitionagainst waiver by agents.
Afterthedeath of the insured,payment of the policywas
contested .
Held : That evidence leaves no doubt of the authority
ofAllison to make the contract and accept a note due in
ninety days in lieu of cash for the first premium . It was
not only within the apparent scope of his authority , but
appellanthad impliedly conferred it by ratifyinga num-
ber of otherriskshe had written in thisway previously.
When the insured made his application and gave his notes ,

accepted as payment , the contractwas then closed,as the

Case 112. Park v. Hilton, 21 R. 1319 ; 54 S. W. 949
(1900).
Burnam ,J., reversing.
Forfeiture- Non-paymentofpremiumnote. The policy pro-
videdthatifany premium or any note given for premiums
shouldnotbe paid on or before the days on which they
became due, the policy should then become void and the
insurance cease, without notice to the insured . A note
givenin payment of a premium on such a policy was not
paidwhen due, and was returned by the company to its
localagent. Shortly thereafterthe company revoked the
agent'sauthorityto act for it, but after such revocation
the agent accepted a renewal note given for the former
note. Held : That as the policy became void for non-
payment, and as the agent had no authority to accept re-
newal , the note was without consideration and the maker
was not liable thereon .
Case 113. Pollockv.Smith, 107 Ky .509 ; 21 R. 1227 ;
54 S.W. 740 (1900).
Hobson , J., affirming.
Equity- Pledge- Lien. Pollock took out two policies on
hislife, each for $5,000. He was indebted to Smith for
$7,000. To secure the payment of the same ,he assigned
thepoliciesto Smith and the assignment alsocovered any
premiums which Smith should thereafter pay . Smith
paidthepremiums untilthe death of Pollock on December
11,1896. The assignment had been made on November 2 ,
1876,and on April 9, 1877. The insurance company paid
intocourt the sum of $9,689.25. Pollock'sadministrator,
resistingthe claim of Smith , claimed that the debt of $7,-
000was barredby limitationand that only premiums paid
within fiveyears could be taken out of the insurance
money by Smith . This defense was denied below and
Smith recoveredon the debt, interestand premiums .
Held : Such payment was proper, although it is well
settledin this State that a mortgage or lien is but an in-
cidentof the debt and disappears when the debt isbarred,
yet a vendee or pledgee will not be required to release

was that Smith was to get his money out of theproceeds
of the insurance policiesat the death of Pollock, andto
hold that he was barred by limitationrunning inthelife-
time of Pollock would be to destroy the very rightthat
the parties contracted for.

Deathinviolationoflaw. The policy provided that if the

Constructionof policy. The tenets established for the
guidance of courts are well understood, and no one is
betterestablishedthan that in all cases the policy must
be liberallyconstrued in favor of the assured so as not to
defeat,without a plain necessity,his claim for indemnity.
And when thewords used may ,withoutviolence,be given
two interpretations,that which willsustainthe claim and
cover the loss should be adopted .
Incontestableclause- Death in violationof law. The fourth
clauseof the policy provides that it shallbe void "ifthe
insureddies in consequence of his own criminal action".
The twelfthclause of the policy reads as follows: "If
theinsuredshalldie three or more years after the date
hereofand after all due premiums shall have been re-
ceivedby the company , this policyshall be incontest-
able." Suit having been brought on the policy afterthe
deathof the insured,the company defended on the ground
that the insured assaulted a man and was killed in con-
sequenceof his own criminal action in making the assault
and that, therefore, the policy became void. Premiums
formore than threeyears had been paid before the death,
butthecompany contendedthatthisviolationof thelaw

INSURANCE LAW OF KENTUCKY .

was not one of the causes covered by the incontestable
clause, and that that clause was intended to preventcon-
testfor misrepresentationsin securingthe policy. Held:
That the language providing that the policyshouldbein-
contestabledoes not restrictitto any particulargrounds
of contest, but it is broad and comprehensiveenoughto
embrace any and every defense which might havebeen
made to itbefore the expirationof threeyears. By the
incontestablefeatureof the policy, the company ineffect
said itwould not refuse to pay the amount of thepolicy
although the insured might ,while sane or insane,takehis
own life,or ifhe should loseitby his own criminalaction.
It was not an agreement that it would pay him the
amount of the policy if he committed suicideor losthis

that no defense should be made on that ground ifhecon-
tinued to pay the premiums for three years.

Retentionofnotes. The retentionof the notesby appel-
lantcompany , as explained by it, was in no wise incon-

R. 1443; Ib.,443 ;22 R. 875.)
Notice. No noticeof the lapsingof a policyis required

Estoppel. That the insured misconstrued the effect of
thenote of June 20th from the local agent or relied on such
misconstruction would not be sufficient to create waiver or
extendthe time by estoppel. The insurer must have in-
tendedsuch result,or the paper itselfmust be reasonably
susceptibleto such construction, before the insured could
inlaw take the riskof assuming that a further extension
was therebygranted him ; that the insurer retained the
notes, as explained by it, and that the insured retained
thepolicies,can not be held to amount to a waiver of the
forfeiture.
Retentionofpolicy- Demand of return. The company had
no legalrightto demand a return of the policies, as the
assuredhad a rightupon certainconditionsto demand a
reinstatement of his insurance under them and the com-
pany had the right to retain the notes, marking them
"canceled" as evidence of their non -payment , for which
itsliabilityupon the policywas void. Thereafter, all
thecompany had to do was to remain silentor inactive
in the matter .

Deliveryof policy- Good health- Optionon policy. Appellee,

ofthecompany ,appelleeclaiming that an option for sixty

continuancein good health." Between the date of the

from them at the time he tendered the premium .

mine whether or not he would accept same did not have

goodhealth when firstpremium was paid. The court be-

the policy.

Actionon policy- Parolevidenceas tomortalitytables. In an

ant did not deny the allegation. Held : That a copy of

icyprovidingfor extended insurance is silentas to the

cent of the amounts retained for a guaranty fund accord-

surance .

Case 165. Scott'sAdmr .v.Scott,25 R.1356;77 S.W.
1122 (1904 ).
Settle,J., affirming.
InsurableInterest- Wageringpolicy. The beneficiaryof a
lifeinsurance policy, which was obtainedby decedentvol-
untarily for her benefit, had in good faithmarried him
in the beliefthat he had been divorced from his firstwife,
and livedwith him many years withouteverbeingin-
formed that there was even a doubt of the legalityofher
marriage untilafterhisdeath, though hisfirstwifeand
her daughters had every opportunity to adviseher of it
long before his death. Besides this, she and her chil-
dren were absolutelydependent on decedent forsupport.
Held that, independent of the validityof her marriage,
she had an insurableinterestin his life,and thatthecon-
tract could not be regarded as within the ruleagainst
wagering policies.
Righttoproceeds- Divorceand remarriage. In an action to
determine the right to lifeinsurance itwas conclusively
shown that deceased had separated from his firstwife
more than 15 years before his death; that he leftthe
State avowedly to live in another State long enough to
get a divorce; that following his return aftera longab-
sence he married a second wife, and afterher deatha
third,in whose favorthe policywas drawn ;thathe lived
with them for more than 30 years, publiclyrecognizing
each in turn ; that the firstwife, who continuedto reside
in the same place during that time, livednear enough to
meet the second before her death and to see the third
often, and was never known to deny that he had been
divorcedfrom her,or to make complaintofhismarriage
to either; and,in additionto that,she permittedtwoof
her daughters to live some time with their father and his
third wife. Held , That the presumption of the legality
of the last marriage created by this showing shiftedto
the firstwife and others claiming with her the burdenof
establishing its legality.

policy, on demand thereforwithin six months, for as

surancecompany is incorporated, which statuteis then

tionof the statute on the contract is excluded by the
terms of the contract .

terestin thelifeof hischild,and a childinthelifeofhis

sonableground , founded upon the relationsof theparties

the lifeof the assured. Otherwise,the contractisa mere
wager,by which theparty takingthepolicyisdeeplyin-

laws as to lifecompanies .
Company heldnot a charitableassociation. The Mutual Re-
serve Fund Life Association is not a charitableassocia-

This istrue,though itsobjectisstatedas themutual bene-
fitof itsmembers ,and though itdisposesof profitsby des
ignatingthem salaries,feesand expenses.

Held : That under the charter after the death of the

name another beneficiaryas the one he did name isthe

Held : That the Financier of the Lodge to whom the

Expulsionof member- Restoration- Power of courts. It ap
pearedby the amended answer that the appellant,having
beenexpelledfrom a benefitsociety,appealed to a higher
court,as provided by the rules of the Order , and was ad-
judged to be restoredas a member . The societyrefused
to obey the order and excluded him from all privileges
and benefits of the association . The Court held : The case
then stands with the appellant a member of the associa-
tionwho has paid his dues and is interestedin the fund
and stilldenied the rightto enjoy any of itsbenefits. The
Chancellor will not or can not restore him to member-
shipor requirethat he shallbe permitted to attend their
statedmeetings, but he can say that this corporation is
the trustee for its members and holds the money for the
benefitof each and every member for the objects con-
templatedby the charter, and he should withhold the re-
liefby refusingjudgment for the appellee lodge until
completeequity is done appellant. The specificreliefto451

Beneficiary- Changeof- Assignmentof policy. Where the
charter of a societyprovides that the widows and chil-
dren of the members shallbe the beneficiaries,a member
can not defeat the claims of such beneficiariesby an as-
signment of his policy to a creditor. The factthatthe
charter gives a member the right to provide in what pro-

Assignment of policy- Right of personwho can notbe assignee.
Where a policyhas been assignedto a personwho cannot
be a beneficiarythereunder, while he is not entitledto

will reimburse him for what he has paid in assessments
and in making proof of death .

Case 199. KentuckyLodge No. 39 1.0. 0. F.v. Lime-
back,8 R.705 (1887). Supr.Ct.

Members bound by tribunals- Sick benefits. The members of
a voluntaryassociationcan not appeal to the courts, but
forthe assertionof theirrights must seek the tribunals
createdwithin the association, and are bound by the de-
cisions of those tribunals. The question as to whether
a member isentitledto sick benefitsunder the by -laws is
to be determined by the Noble Grand , a principalofficer
of the lodge, with a right of appeal to the lodge and then
to thegrand lodge of the State. The plaintiffapplied to
theNoble Grand for sickbenefits, who refusedhim . This
suitwas then brought without appealing to the lodge or
to thegrand lodge. Held : That the courts will not hear
his complaint .
Actionforbenefits- Pleading. The defendant having al-
leged in its answer that certain rules and regulations
which were filedhad been adopted by the lodge, plaintiff
denied that the defendant had furnished any tribunal for
determiningthe rightof a member to sickbenefits. Held :
This was not a denial of the adoption of the rules and
was pleading a conclusion or construction concerning
the rules .

5 S.W. 385 ;9 Am .St.Rep .270 (1887).
Holt,J., affirming.
Beneficiaries- Changeof- Regulations. The regulation as to
mode of changing beneficiarymust be strictlyfollowed if
insistedupon by the association, but the associationmay
waive such compliance and pay the new beneficiaryif
convinced that the change was intended by the insured ,
who signifiedhis intentionby a written directionto the
association. The appellee, the Ancient Order of United
Workmen , issuedto one Manning , through itsSupreme
Lodge, a certificateof membership entitlinghim to an
interestin itsbeneficiaryfund to the extent of $2,000, to
be paid at his death to his brother. Before his death,
the insureddirected the policy to be made out to Mrs.

JosieA. Manning . Uuder the organic law of the Order,
members had a right to change the beneficiary. The com-
pany paid Mrs. Manning . The brother named firstas
beneficiarysued on the ground that the writtendirection
to change the beneficiarywas not a compliance with the
form and the regulationprescribedby the Order forsuch
changes, and further providing that, unless saidregula-
tionwas compliedwith, the change would notbevalid.
The court held that the objectof the regulationwas to
prevent the Order from becoming involved in litigation
with outside claimants, but that the societyhad a right
eitherto insistupon the protectionof exact compliance
with the regulationor waive it; that the insured,having
manifested his intentionto change and having directedthe
change in writing, and the Order having paid thebene-
ficiarylastdesignated,no other person had a righttocom-
plain.

Application- Parol evidencenot allowed to vary. Where a writ-
tenapplicationisthe basis of a contractof lifeinsurance,

Beneficiaries- Changeof- Vested rights. A. had certificatesin

empowered to trade as a feme sole. Becoming indebted,
he in his lastillnesscanceled those certificates,andtook

ing from the charters of the associations that theirchief

able to some other person .

Case204. Wilsonv.Wright,8 R.963 (1887).
Barbour, J., affirming.
Liabilityof officeron bond - Running accounts- Applicationof
payments. F. was elected"Master of Exchequer " of a lodge
of Knights of Pythias in 1879, and annually thereafter
until1885. Although the constitution of this order re-
quiredthatthisofficershould give bond untilApril,1884,
when he executedthe bond sued on, the obligorscovenant-
ingthathe would render an account forallmoney or other
propertythat should come , or had already come , to his
hands,"or is now in his hands ." At the time he executed
the bond he owed the lodge from $ 400 to $500 . He had
placedthe money in his business, but this fact was not
known eitherto the lodge or to his sureties. When his
successorwas electedin 1885 he owed the lodge $880.17,
of which he afterwards paid $500 . This is an action on
his bond to recover the balance . After his election in
1884, and while stillin office, he paid out and received
credit for more than $ 880 .
Held , First. F. was at least a de facto officerprior to
the time he executed bond , and the suretiescan not rely
upon his failure to execute bond as a defense as to the
money thatcame to his hands during that time.
Second . The order being a voluntary association, the
court must assume that the lodge had the power to alter
or amend the constitution so as to allow officersto act,
though they might not execute bond as that instrument
required.
Third. What F. owed to the lodge when the bond was
executed was , in legal contemplation, "in his hands "
withinthe meaning of the bond , although the money was
investedin his business, and his suretiesare liablethere-
for.
Fourth . Even if the sureties were not liable for the
pastacts of F., as he, during the year 1884, paid more
thanhe owed when electedfor that year, and no appli-
cationofthosepayments had been made ,thecourthad the
righttoapplythem to the past indebtedness, and having
ineffectdone so,thejudgment willnot be disturbed.
Fifth. When there are long -running accounts , and
debitsand creditsare perpetuallyoccurring,and no bal-

ances are otherwise adjustedthan for themere purposeof
making rests,payments ought to be appliedtoextinguish
the debts according to the priorityof time.

Recovery of premium paid under mistake. Under the charter

nate as beneficiarya relation, but insteadof providing
