Assessments- Actionon policy- Pleading- Conclusionof law. It
willbe seenthatitisallegedin substance that therewere
members enough in said company holding lifecertificates,
had they been assessedaccording to the graded plan of
assessment, to have paid the maximum amount of the ap-
pellee'spolicy, and the appellant is calledupon to state
the number of persons subject to assessment and the
amount of the same . The appellantfailedto answer this-
interrogatory,but statedthat itdid make assessments to
pay thisand eightother policiesand the same aggregated
thesum of $29,500, which was equitablyprorated among
thenine policies. These allegationsdid not respond to
theinterrogatoriesand the allegationsof the petitionin
referenceto that matter. Besides, the allegationsof the
answerin referenceto said matter are wholly evasive. It
isnot stated what amount was assessed to pay the appel-
lee'spolicy, or what amount was assessed to pay either
of the other policies, or what was the amount of either
policy. The statement that the appellees received as
much as they were equitably entitled to receivewas a
mere conclusionof the pleader. It is certain, according
to the allegationsof the pleadings, that no certificate
could have been issued for an amount exceeding three
thousand dollars, but could have been issued for a less
amount;and presuming that each of the nine certificates
calledfor three thousand dollars as the maximum amount ,
the aggregatesum would be twenty -seven thousand dol-
lars, and the answer admits that twenty -nine thousand
fivehundred dollarswas assessedon said deaths, appar-
entlyenough to pay the maximum sum of each certificate
in full. Why only eight hundred dollarswas the appel-

faras appears from the pleadings, had no right to pro-
ratethe sum raised by the assessments. The appellees,
as faras appears from the pleadings, were entitledto an
assessmenton allthe members subject to it for the pur-
pose of paying the amount of their policy, and each of
the others was entitled to an assessment for the same
purpose, and it was not right to prorate the sums thus
raisedamong the beneficiaries.

risk that the insured shall become insane and killhim-

Materiality. A plea relying upon a misrepresentation
must show not only that the fact misrepresented in-
creasedthe danger of hazard of loss, but that, according
to the rules or modes of business of the insurers, it would
have enhanced the premium or prevented the insurance ,
and thus the insurers were misled by the representa-
tion .
Pleading. In alleging that a fire place was insecure
contrary to a representation, it must be particularly
statedin what the insecurityconsisted, so as to apprise
the insured of the facts relied on to avoid the policy. And
the word "fraudulent" applied to the representationor
concealment, does not magnify or even identifythe fact
misrepresentedor concealed and can not supply the omis-
sion to show its materiality.
Pleading. Where the charter of an insurance company
providesthat unless the insured disclosesin his policy
thetruenature of the titleto his property the policyshall
be void, the insurer,in taking advantage of such stipula-
tions, should allegespecificallythe true titleof the in-
suredand show that itsvalue is materiallylessthan the
valueof the representedtitle.
Increasedhazardfrom kitchen- Risk- Dwellinghouse. A dwell-
ing house is more in danger of being consumed or in
danger by fireifone of itsrooms be used as a kitchen than
ifno such use were made of any room in it,but the kitchen
was in another room unconnected with it.
Representations- Description- Dwelling house. The repre-
sentationof a house as a dwelling house is not falsified
by averment and proof that one of itsrooms was used as a
kitchen; and a statement in the survey that there was a
kitchenand negro building in the rear is not entirely
equivalentto a representationthat there was no kitchen
in the dwelling house.

liento secure the conveyance of the real estate. But if

extent diminished , and the insurer is entitled to have a
deductionfrom his liabilityby a diminution of the dam-
ages to the extent of the value of such real estate as fixed
in the contract .

Representations— Fraud- Overvaluation. If the insured in-
nocently,and in good faith, over-valueshisproperty, his

Case 300. Mayes v. HartfordFireIns. Co.,3 R. 687
(1882).
Pryor, J., affirming.

invalidatethe insurance policiesin this case, notwith-
standing the fact that the insurance agent knew them to

(1883). Supr.Ct.
Reid,J., affirming.
Forfeiture- Non-paymentof premiums. A provision that

by the terms of the installment note" is valid and bind-
ing on the insured .
The contract- Publicpolicy. The parties to a contract of

contraryto law or public policy.
See Case 302 herein .

Richards,J.,reversing.
Acts of agents. Insurance companies are liable for the
actsand representationsof theiragents,whenever parties

dealing with them are justifiedin supposing the agent
was authorized to do the act or make the representations
in question .
The insured has the right to rely upon instructions
given him by the agent issuingthe policyas to whom and
where the premiums shall be paid, the contractbeing
silentupon the subject.
The insured in this case had the right to relyupon
the directionof the agent not to pay the premiums in
any other way than as directedby the generalagentin
Chicago , from whom the agent informed him he would

directinghim how , where and to whom he shouldmake

directionon the back of the policy to addressallcom-
munications relativeto the policyto a certainpersonwas
not a part of the contract or a directionto make thepay-
ments to that person .
The insured, failingto receive any directionas to
whom he should make the payments , isnot in defaultfor
his failure to make them .

4 R.706 (1883). Supr.Ct.
Reid , J., affirming.
Personalproperty- Situsof moveableproperty- Buggies- Warranty.
Where two buggies had been insured with otherproperty

pairs,itwas heldthatthe referenceto thebuggiesasbe-

to merchandise, etc., placed in a store.

Ownershipof personalproperty- Conclusivenessof judgment as to-
Judgment- Personalproperty. An infant bought some goods

The sellerof the goods brought suit for the purchase

defended, but judgment was rendered against him and

againstthe company that the judgment affecting the

not be again litigated.

Where a policyof fireinsurancecontainsthe condition

any otherinsurance on the property hereby insured," the

on the same property, even though the policy subse-

quently issued be void. Id. A recoveryon the first

When a policy of fireinsurance describestheprop-
erty as a two-storybrick,shingleroofbuilding,occupied

building, he can recover on the policy.

arbitratorsthe question of amount of lossand thearbi

tingout thisfactauthorizesthe courttorenderjudgment
forthe amount of thepolicy,such amount beinglessthan
the award .

resentation, though untrue, will not avoid a policy un-

sentedthat she owned the property in fee simple, when

Assignmentof policy- Waiver. The insured sold his prop-
erty and asked the company's solicitorto assign the
policyto the purchaser. The solicitortold him to apply
to the superintendent, which he did, sending the superin-
tendentthe policywith the assignment endorsed thereon
forapproval. The superintendent sent a blank form to
theinsuredto be filledout by the purchaser, saying, that
upon receiptof the form properly filledout, the company
would give the matter prompt attention. The insured
neverreceivedthe letterand , on inquiringof the solicitor
the cause of the delay, was told that it was caused by

the blank form referred to . The court held that there

tive thereto .

Bowden , J., reversing.

when sent to him , and to collectthe premiums , is not

Transferof title- Levy of execution.Where a firepolicy

edge of the agent is knowledge of the company . Where

has fullknowledge of all facts material to the risk,the

of the company and the mistakeof the agentcannotbe

scriptionas to occupancy . Under such circumstances,

Writtenmattercontrolsprinted. In a fireinsurance policy

ing.

powder and coal-oil were usually kept in country stores

ment .

Parol evidence of verbal instructions given the insured

Parol contractto insure- Incomplete contract- Mistake- Estoppel.
An agent who represented several insurance companies
had a conversationwith insured regarding the insurance
on the latter'sproperty. The agent got out his book,
from which it appeared, upon an examination by both
parties, that policiesin two of the companies had ex-
piredand thatpoliciesin four othersof the companies
remained in force. The insured instructed the agent to
renew the two policieswhich had expired, which was

done . The insured also testifiedthat he toldtheagent
to keep up $12,000 worth of insuranceon theproperty.
Shortly afterwards a fireoccurred, and itwas discov

existenceof the four policies, which had in factexpired.
It was held that this evidence did not constitute an oral
contract to insure. And although the insuredtestified
that he would then have directed the renewal of the
policiesbut for the agent'srepresentationsthattheywere
stillin existence, it was held that the company wasnot
estopped by the agent'sacts.
Contract- Parol contract. A complete contract must be

lishsuch a contract by a preponderance of evidence.
Pleading. In a suit against several insurancecom-
panies for the same loss, one of the companiesfileda

in the proofs of loss. To thisanswer the insuredfailed
to reply. On the trialof the case,however,itappeared
that no material misstatements had been made inthe
proofsof loss. The Court heldthat,whileundertheCode
the answer must be taken as true for failuretodenyits
allegations, stillthe lower court shouldallowthein-
sured a reasonable time to filea reply to theanswerupon
return of the case .

such real estate, personal estate, choses in action,etc..
within the city,or belonging"to theinhabitantsthereof,"

as may be "taxableby the laws of this Commonwealth "

Taxonsurplusofinsurancecompany. In estimating the
surplusof an insurance company for the purpose of tax-
ationunder the equalizationlaw , it is proper to deduct

ticular fact .

inthepolicyprovidingthat itshould be void for any mis-

defeatedifno evidence were given on eitherside." And

theopening." Under thisstateof the record, the burden
ofproofwas on the defendant and ithad the right to the
concludingargument to the jury.

Lease. One Hawse solicitedfrom appelleeinsuranceon

lant, who insured the distilleryin appellantcompany.
Hawse knew nothing of the company, had no relations

not know in what company theyinsuredtheproperty.It
appears that contrary to policyconditionstheappel-
lee was not the absoluteowner of the distilleryproperty,

distillerywas not then in operation. Hawse knewthese
facts. Rudd & Son to whom he brought theriskdidnot
know them .
Held : That the solicitor,Hawse ,who was ineffectthe
partner of Rudd & Son in effectingthisinsurance,must

the premium and insuredthe property. The knowledge

Forfeiturefor failureto furnish not implied.
Where the policy does not provide that the failureto

Co.v.Weiss,109 Ky.464;22 R.994;59 S.W. 509 (1900).
Burnam , J., affirming.
Valuedpolicylaw- Totalloss. "It is the opinion of the
courtthatthe words 'totalloss,' when applied to a build-
ing,do not mean that the materials of which the build-
ingwas composed were alltotallydestroyedand obliter-
ated. It isnot necessary that allthe parts and materials
composingthebuildingshouldbe absolutelyand physic-
allydestroyed,but theinquiryalways is,doesthe insured
buildingafterthe firestillexist,preservingsubstantially
itsidentity,or has itbecome so broken and disintegrated
thatitcan not be designated as the structurewhich was
insured. There may be a totalloss within the meaning
of the statuteeven though some parts of the building
may remain standing after the fire." In this case the
evidenceas to the conditionof the buildingisconflicting,
and the factswere submitted to the jury under a proper
construction of the statute as outlined above , and the
jury found for the plaintiffand the judgment thereon
will not be disturbed .

selfhad notice of the additional insurance . Thus a good
cause of action is stated .

REINSURANCE-
1. Original Policy Holders ' Rights Against Reinsuring Company .
Reinsuring company waives written acceptance by insured
of transferred risk by notifying insured of premiums due
and accepting payment of them . People's Mut . Assur .
Fund v . Boesse , 92 Ky . 290 ; 13 R. 660 ; 17 S. W. 630 .... 49
A letter of a policy holder in the reinsured company express-
ing desire for transfer of insurance is an acceptance
in advance of insurance in the reinsuring company . Id . 49
II. Agents ' Powers as to Reinsurance .
Where an agent for two companies attempts to write insur-
ance in one company on property insured in the other ,
ostensibly for insured , but really for a secret indemnity
of the other principal , such act is not binding on the
companies . London & Lan . Fire Ins. Co. v . Turnbull ,
86 Ky . 230 ; 9 R. 544 ; 5 S. W. 542
III. Avoidance or Forfeiture of Contract .
Where the reinsuring company , on application therefor by a
policy holder in the reinsured company , issued a policy
to such applicant , referring to his former application
and making it a part of the contract , this is a trans-
ferred risk and is not avoided by misrepresentation of
the original policy holder made at the time of reinsur-
ance . People's Mut . Assur . Fund v . Boesse , 92 Ky . 290 ;
13 R. 660 ; 17 S. W. 630
Where a reinsuring company , in an action to settle the af-
fairs of a reinsured company , recovered from the latter
for premiums received , but not earned on account of the
transfer, including the insurance in question , this showed
conclusively that this was transferred insurance , and
not avoided by misrepresentations of original policy-
holder at the time of reinsurance . Id .
IV . Change of Plan of Insurance .
A company doing business on assessment plan can not , with-
out consent of members , abandon that plan and adopt
" straight insurance system ." People's Mut . Ins. Fund v .
Bricken , 92 Ky . 297
A policyholder is not obliged to retain his policy, but may
rescind and sue for premiums . Id .
REJECTION-
Rejection after loss where company bound by agreement does
not affect the insured . Howard Ins . Co. v . Owen's Admx .,
94 Ky . 197 ; 14 R. 881 ; 21 S. W. 1037

Rejection- Misrepresentationin Application. The insured in his
application for insurance on his lifein answer to the ques-
tion as to whether he had made application to another
company for insurance, and had that applicationrefused,
repliedno , when in fact his applicationhad been refused
by another company . In an action on said policy, the in-
sured having committed suicide,held,that said representa-
tionwas immaterial, as itappears thatthe examining phy-
sician discovered the same trouble that was the basis of
the former company's refusal to accept the application,
but after a second examination by the examining phy-
sician, with the concurrence in opinion of another phy-
sician, the application was accepted .
